revision.text:
{{Infobox person|image=File:Nitta Yoshisada.jpg|birth_date=1301|death_date={{death date|1338|8|17}}|birth_place={{ill|Hōsen|WD=Q11452597|1=Hōsen}}; {{ill|Ikushina|WD=Q11574471|1=Ikushina}}; {{ill|Satomi|WD=Q11644677|1=Satomi}}|death_place={{ill|Tomyo-ji Nawate: The Site of Yoshisada Nitta's Death|WD=Q11569246|1=Tōmyōji Nawate}}|gender={{ill|male|WD=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Japan|WD=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|bushi|WD=Q1975935|1=bushi}}; {{ill|samurai|WD=Q38142|1=Samurai}}|spouse={{ill|Kōtōnonaishi|WD=Q11400637|1=Kōtōnonaishi}}|children={{ill|Nitta Yoshiaki|WD=Q3342237|1=Nitta Yoshiaki}}; {{ill|Nitta Yoshimune|WD=Q3342232|1=Nitta Yoshimune}}; {{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|1=Nitta Yoshioki}}; {{ill|Q106696963|WD=Q106696963|1=Q106696963}}|father={{ill|Nitta Tomouji|WD=Q11503735|1=Nitta Tomouji}}|mother={{ill|Horiguchi Sadayoshi's foster daughter|WD=Q106681514|1=Horiguchi Sadayoshi's foster daughter}}|family={{ill|Nitta clan|WD=Q2274150|1=Nitta clan}}|native_name=新田義貞|given_name={{ill|Yoshisada|WD=Q123679403|1=Yoshisada}}|resting_place={{ill|Shōnen-ji|WD=Q11596188|1=Shōnen-ji Temple}}}} {{Short description|Japanese samurai lord (1301–1338)}} {{family name hatnote|Nitta|lang=Japanese}} [[File:Nitta_Yoshisada.jpg|right|thumb|Nitta Yoshisada]] {{nihongo|'''Nitta Yoshisada'''|新田 義貞||1301 – August 17, 1338}} also known as '''Minamoto no Yoshisada''' was a {{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai|1=Samurai}} lord of the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|en|Nanboku-chō period|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Nanboku-chō period}} Japan. He was the head of the {{ill|Nitta clan|en|Nitta clan|WD=Q2274150|lt=Nitta clan|1=Nitta clan}} in the early fourteenth century, and supported the Southern Court of {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} in the Nanboku-chō period. He famously marched on {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, besieging and capturing it from the {{ill|Hōjō clan|en|Hōjō clan|WD=Q834364|lt=Hōjō clan|1=Hōjō clan}} in 1333. Later, he fought the Ashikaga brothers on the Emperor's behalf in a see-saw campaign which saw the capital change hands several times. After a peaceful compromise was agreed, Yoshisada was entrusted with two royal princes. At the {{ill|Siege of Kanegasaki (1337)|en|Siege of Kanegasaki (1337)|WD=Q7510148|lt=Siege of Kanegasaki (1337)|1=Siege of Kanegasaki (1337)}}, both princes were killed, along with Yoshisada's son, although Yoshisada was able to escape. He committed {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku|1=Seppuku}} when his horse was killed at the [[:en:siege of Kuromaru|siege of Kuromaru]]. == Early life == Yoshisada was born in 1301, the eldest son of Nitta Tomouji. He succeeded his father and became the lord of Nitta Manor in {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke Province|1=Kōzuke Province}} in 1317. At this time, he also became the head of the {{ill|Nitta clan|en|Nitta clan|WD=Q2274150|lt=Nitta clan|1=Nitta clan}}.{{Cite book |url=https://www.worldcat.org/oclc/33014222 |title=Asashi Nihon rekishi jinbutsu jiten |publisher=Asahi Shinbunsha |others=Asahi Shinbunsha, 朝日新聞社. |year=1994 |isbn=4-02-340052-1 |at=新田義貞 |oclc=33014222}} Yoshisada courted a daughter of a court noble, Kōtō-Naishi (匂当内侍), and married her through the emperor's mediation.{{rp|65}} ==Kamakura campaign== Long an enemy of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, Nitta Yoshisada is often blamed for the split between the [[:en:Nanboku-cho|Northern and Southern Courts]], as he fought against the {{ill|Ashikaga clan|en|Ashikaga clan|WD=Q725370|lt=Ashikaga|1=Ashikaga clan}} and for the emperor, {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}. This rivalry came largely from the fact that the Ashikaga were Ranked above the Nitta, despite their being descended from a younger ancestor; since the ancestors of the Nitta did not fight alongside their [[Minamoto clan|Minamoto]] cousins in the {{ill|Genpei War|en|Genpei War|WD=Q737542|lt=Genpei War|1=Genpei War}}, they were never accorded power or prestige at Kamakura. ===March on Kamakura=== {{main|Siege of Kamakura (1333)}} In 1331, after being ordered by the ''{{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=bakufu|1=Ashikaga shogunate}}'' (shogunate) to join an army at the Chihaya fortress, Nitta was ordered by [[:en:Prince Morinaga|Prince Morinaga]] and {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} to strike at the Hōjō, so he left his post. Returning to his home province of Kozuke, Yoshisada rallied the aid of other descendants and vassals, including his brother {{ill|Nitta Yoshisuke|en|Nitta Yoshisuke|WD=Q969116|lt=Yoshisuke|1=Nitta Yoshisuke}} of the Minamoto clan, and began to [[:en:Kōzuke-Musashi Campaign|march toward Kamakura]] through {{ill|Musashi Province|en|Musashi Province|WD=Q907398|lt=Musashi|1=Musashi Province}}. On the approaches to the city, Nitta enjoyed some early victories, routing the Hōjō defenders and pursuing them towards the city. [[File:Yoshitoshi_-_100_Aspects_of_the_Moon_-_39.jpg|right|thumb|Nitta Yoshisada offering his sword to [[Ryūjin]] ]] Except for its coastline, Kamakura is surrounded by steep hills, making an overland attack difficult. Nitta first tried to enter through the {{ill|Kamakura's Seven Entrances|en|Kamakura's Seven Entrances|WD=Q1091263|lt=Gokurakuji Pass and the Kewaizaka Pass|1=Kamakura's Seven Entrances}}, but strong Hōjō forces stopped him. Judging it's impossible to enter by land, Nitta decided to try by sea, bypassing {{ill|Inamuragasaki|en|Inamuragasaki|WD=Q11596647|lt=Inamuragasaki|1=Inamuragasaki}} Cape on {{ill|Sagami Bay|en|Sagami Bay|WD=Q1061221|lt=Sagami Bay|1=Sagami Bay}}, west of {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}. Once there, Nitta took advantage of a low tide and moved his men in through the beaches to the south, but according to the ''[[Taiheiki]]'', he threw his sword into the surf and prayed to [[Ryūjin]], who parted the waters for him.In describing this event, Japanese sources say Nitta Yoshisada prayed to a sea-god or [[Ryūjin]]; English sources almost always refer to Sun Goddess Amaterasu. See ''Taiheiki'' ([http://www.j-texts.com/taihei/tk010.html 稲村崎成干潟事]). ''Taiheiki'' states, {{quote|Dismounting from his horse, Yoshisada removed his helmet and prostrating himself across the distant seas prayed to [[Ryūjin]]. "It is said that the lord of Japan from the beginning, [[Amaterasu|Amaterasu Ōmikami]], enshrined at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Jingū]], hid herself within a {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|WD=Q239847|lt=Vairocana|1=Vairocana}} and appeared as Ryūjin of the vast blue seas. My lord ({{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}}) is her descendant, and drifts upon waves of the western sea due to rebels. I Yoshisada, in an attempt to serve as a worthy subject, will pick up my axes and face the enemy line. That desire is to aid the nation and bring welfare to the masses. Ryūjin of the Eight Protectorate Gods of the (seven) Inner Seas and the Outer Sea, witness this subject's loyalty and withdraw the waters afar, open a path to the lines of the three armies.}} He therefore speaks to Ryūjin who, he has heard, is a manifestation of Amaterasu.Mutsu (1995:247) The stele at {{nihongo|Sode no Ura|袖の浦}}, the tiny bay west of Inamuragaki, says:Original Japanese text available [http://www.kcn-net.org/sisekihi/inamura.htm here]
666 years ago on May 21, 1333Old Japanese lunar calendar date. Gregorian date obtained directly from the original Nengō using [https://uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm Nengocalc] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930014735/http://www.uni-tuebingen.de/geschichte-japans/nengo_calc.htm |date=2007-09-30 }}: ''July 3, 1333 ([[:en:Genkō (second)|Genkō]] 3, 21st day of the 5th month)'' Nitta Yoshisada, judging an invasion on land to be difficult, decided to try to bypass this cape. This is the place where, according to tradition, he threw his golden sword into the waves, praying the sea-god to withdraw them and let him pass. (Erected in 1917)
===Fall of Kamakura=== The city was taken, and the Hōjō clan's influence destroyed. Following the fall of Kamakura (and of the Hōjō regency), Yoshisada was appointed governor of {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo|1=Echigo Province}} and vice-governor of {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima|1=Harima Province}} and {{ill|Kōzuke Province|en|Kōzuke Province|WD=Q1054838|lt=Kōzuke|1=Kōzuke Province}} Provinces, as {{ill|Emperor Go-Daigo|en|Emperor Go-Daigo|WD=Q313942|lt=Emperor Go-Daigo|1=Emperor Go-Daigo}} redistributed the Hōjō lands.{{Cite book |last=Sansom |first=George |title=A History of Japan, 1334–1615 |publisher=Stanford University Press |year=1961 |isbn=0804705259 |pages=18–21, 26–27}} He took the {{ill|Seiwa Genji|en|Seiwa Genji|WD=Q2164991|lt=Seiwa Genji|1=Seiwa Genji}} heirloom Higekiri and the {{ill|Tenka-Goken|en|Tenka-Goken|WD=Q9973300|lt=Tenka-Goken|1=Tenka-Goken}} Onimaru Kunitsuna. ==Later campaigns== [[File:Nitta Yoshisada - Tsuchiya Koitsu.jpg|thumb|Nitta Yoshisada fighting bravely with the two swords Onikiri and Onimaru at the Battle of Minatogawa (1336)]] During the following few years, Nitta Yoshisada's rivalry with {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and his brother {{ill|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|en|Ashikaga Tadayoshi|WD=Q725375|lt=Ashikaga Tadayoshi|1=Ashikaga Tadayoshi}} came to a head, with an imperial commission to destroy the two brothers issued in 1335. The two armies fought a number of battles, starting at the Yahagi River on December and ending at Mishima later that month. Yoshisada's forces were eventually defeated and the brothers advanced upon Kyoto.{{rp|39–42}} The Ashikagas were able to capture Kyoto for a few days in February 1336, before help arrived for Yoshisada and Kusunoki from {{ill|Emperor Go-Murakami|en|Emperor Go-Murakami|WD=Q440447|lt=Prince Norinaga|1=Emperor Go-Murakami}} and {{ill|Prince Takanaga|en|Prince Takanaga|WD=Q7244257|lt=Prince Takanaga|1=Prince Takanaga}}.{{rp|43–44}} In April 1336 Nitta attacked {{ill|Akamatsu Norimura|en|Akamatsu Norimura|WD=Q2828933|lt=Akamatsu Norimura|1=Akamatsu Norimura}} in {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|WD=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}. He kept up the investment of Akamatsu's strongholds at Shirohata and Mitsuishi until June, when he retreated in the face of advances by Tadayoshi's army.{{rp|49–50}} Yoshisada was defeated in the {{ill|Battle of Minatogawa|en|Battle of Minatogawa|WD=Q114620|lt=Battle of Minatogawa|1=Battle of Minatogawa}} allowing Takauji to occupy Kyoto once again.{{rp|50–52}}{{Cite book |last=Turnbull |first=Stephen |title=The Samurai, A Military History |publisher=MacMillan Publishing Co., Inc. |year=1977 |isbn=0026205408 |pages=101–102}}{{cite book|last=Turnbull|first=Stephen|title=The Samurai Sourcebook |publisher=Cassell & Co |date=1998 |pages=206, 208 |isbn=1-85409-523-4}} Nitta retreated with the emperor to [[:en:Hieizan|Hieizan]]. Eventually, on November 13, 1336, the emperor agreed with Takauji's offers to return to {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}. Before he did so, he entrusted Nitta with escorting {{ill|Prince Takanaga|en|Prince Takanaga|WD=Q7244257|lt=Prince Takanaga|1=Prince Takanaga}} and {{ill|Prince Tsunenaga|en|Prince Tsunenaga|WD=Q3541669|lt=Prince Tsunenaga|1=Prince Tsunenaga}} to {{ill|Echizen Province|en|Echizen Province|WD=Q1047144|lt=Echizen Province|1=Echizen Province}}. They made it as far as a loyalist stronghold, where they had to endure the {{ill|Siege of Kanegasaki (1337)|en|Siege of Kanegasaki (1337)|WD=Q7510148|lt=Siege of Kanegasaki (1337)|1=Siege of Kanegasaki (1337)}}. Nitta escaped, but his son {{ill|Nitta Yoshiaki|en|Nitta Yoshiaki|WD=Q3342237|lt=Nitta Yoshiaki|1=Nitta Yoshiaki}} and the princes were eventually killed.{{rp|54–55,63–64}} ==Death== Nitta's death was as remarkable as his life. While Nitta was fighting in the [[:en:siege of Kuromaru|siege of Kuromaru]] against {{ill|Hosokawa Akiuji|en|Hosokawa Akiuji|WD=Q5908193|lt=Hosokawa Akiuji|1=Hosokawa Akiuji}}, an ally of Takauji, his horse was felled by arrow fire. Nitta, pinned under the dead horse and unable to move, was an easy target for archers. As a final act, Nitta is supposed to have drawn his short sword and cut off his own head. Record has it that a number of his fellow samurai committed ''junshi {{ill|Seppuku|en|Seppuku|WD=Q133462|lt=Seppuku|1=Seppuku}}'' nearby, in a show of allegiance.Turnbull 2003. Yoshisada died on August 17, 1338. ==Honours== * [[List of Japanese court Ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Senior First Rank]] (August 7, 1882; posthumous) ==See also== * [[Fujishima Shrine]] *{{ill|Nitta Yoshiaki|en|Nitta Yoshiaki|WD=Q3342237|lt=Nitta Yoshiaki|1=Nitta Yoshiaki}} - son of Yoshisada *{{ill|Nitta Yoshioki|en|Nitta Yoshioki|WD=Q3342234|lt=Nitta Yoshioki|1=Nitta Yoshioki}} - son of Yoshisada *{{ill|Nitta Yoshimune|en|Nitta Yoshimune|WD=Q3342232|lt=Nitta Yoshimune|1=Nitta Yoshimune}} - son of Yoshisada *[[:en:Wakiya Yoshisuke|Wakiya Yoshisuke]] - brother of Yoshisada, and founder of a branch family of Nitta, called [[Wakiya clan|Wakiya]] ==References== {{Reflist|30em}} ==Further reading== * Turnbull, Stephen (2003). ''Samurai: The World of the Warrior''. Osprey Publishing. * {{cite book | last = Mutsu, Iso | author-link = Iso Mutsu | title = Kamakura. Fact and Legend | publisher = Tuttle Publishing | date = June 1995 | location = Tokyo | isbn = 0-8048-1968-8 }} {{wikidata link|Q1328796|cs|Jošisada Nitta|de|Nitta Yoshisada|en|Nitta Yoshisada|es|Nitta Yoshisada|fa|نیتتا یوشی‌سادا|fi|Nitta Yoshisada|fr|Nitta Yoshisada|id|Nitta Yoshisada|it|Nitta Yoshisada|ja|新田義貞|ko|닛타 요시사다|pl|Yoshisada Nitta|pt|Nitta Yoshisada|ru|Нитта Ёсисада|th|นิตตะ โยชิซาดะ|tr|Nitta Yoshisada|uk|Нітта Йосісада|zh|新田义贞}} [[Category:1338 deaths]] [[Category:Deified Japanese men]] [[Category:Genkō War]] [[Category:Japanese military personnel who died by suicide]] [[Category:Minamoto clan]] [[Category:Nitta clan]] [[Category:People of Kamakura-period Japan]] [[Category:People of Nanboku-chō-period Japan]] [[Category:Samurai]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
